findings.evidence: MD5 (128-bit) — Practical collisions demonstrated by Wang et al. (2004); avoid for all security uses

findings.vulnerability: Deprecated hash algorithm in use: SHA1
findings.evidence: SHA1 (160-bit) — SHAttered attack (2017) produced the first practical SHA-1 collision; deprecated by NIST

findings.vulnerability: Hash verification uses MD5 — susceptible to collision forgery
findings.evidence: MD5 is cryptographically broken — collisions are practical to compute

findings.vulnerability: Birthday attack collision proven on MD5 (32-bit prefix)
findings.evidence: Two distinct inputs ("probe-26232" and "probe-89261") share MD5 prefix 7ba43180 after 89261 attempts. Birthday bound for full 128-bit MD5 is ~2^64 operations.

algorithmSummary.name: md5
algorithmSummary.reason: Practical collisions demonstrated by Wang et al. (2004); avoid for all security uses

algorithmSummary.name: sha1
algorithmSummary.reason: SHAttered attack (2017) produced the first practical SHA-1 collision; deprecated by NIST

algorithmSummary.name: sha256
algorithmSummary.reason: No known practical attacks; current NIST recommendation

collisionDemo.explanation: Birthday attack on 32-bit MD5 prefix: two distinct inputs sharing the same 4-byte hash prefix. Full MD5 hashes differ here, but the same math applies to the full 128-bit space — Wang et al. (2004) exploited MD5's internal structure to achieve full collisions.
